data.text: a building that serves as living quarters for one or a few families: home
examples: invited them to her house for dinner
a two-family house
a carriage house
the house occupied by Jupiter
woke up the whole house
the house of Tudor
a fraternity house
a bill approved by both houses of Congress
a movie house
the local coffee house
a house of worship/God =a church, synagogue, mosque, etc.
runs the restaurant's front of house =dining area
… can no longer require employees to share their tips with the back of house, such as cooks and dishwashers …
a house cocktail =a cocktail whose recipe was created by the bar, restaurant, etc. at which it is served
Save room for a soufflé, the century-long house specialty …
a publishing house
a full house on opening night

data.text: to provide with living quarters or shelter
examples: a place to house their guests
the barn where they house their boat
… so timorous a soul housed in so impressive a body.
buildings that house government offices
a place to house their guests
the barn where they house their boat
… so timorous a soul housed in so impressive a body.
buildings that house government offices

data.text: to encase, enclose, or shelter as if by putting in a house
examples: a place to house their guests
the barn where they house their boat
… so timorous a soul housed in so impressive a body.
buildings that house government offices

data.text: Edward Mandell 1858—1938 Colonel House American diplomat
confinement often under guard to one's house or quarters instead of in prison
a visit (as by a doctor or a repair person) to a home to provide a requested service
a widely distributed cricket (Acheta domesticus) usually living in or about dwellings
a person who is employed (as by a hotel) to prevent disorderly or improper conduct of patrons

either of two widely distributed dust mites (Dermatophagoides farinae and D. pteronyssinus) that commonly occur in house dust and often induce allergic responses (such as runny nose or sneezing) and asthma
examples: … house dust mites—which eat human skin scales—could grow to very large numbers, not only in mattresses and pillows, but also in carpets, upholstered furniture, and even in clothing and window curtains, particularly in warm, humid houses.

data.text: a small finch (Haemorhous mexicanus) that has a male with a red head, breast, and rump and that is native to Mexico and the western U.S. and has been introduced in the eastern U.S.
